segments.text:
Morphine and Related Alkaloids
Heroin , the very addictive illegal drug, is made from morphine, which comes from the opium poppy
It is less polar than morphine, making it more soluble in the body’ s fat cells, more potent
Heroin binds to µ-opioid receptors

Heroin
Heroin and morphine bind to the µ-opioid receptors
Results: euphoria, analgesia (pain relief)
µ-opioid receptors is a GPCR associated with a Gi
Structure of µ-opioid receptors bound to a morphine derivative
Reference: Nature (2012), 485, p321 [https://www.nature.com/nature/journal/v485/n7398/pdf/nature10954.pdf]
Opium and heroin are agonists of the µ-opioid receptors

Agonists & Antagonists, drug interactions
How do agonists elicit an effect?
We can increase the dose of an agonist and look for increasing response or effect
Example: heroin vs. euphoria

Pharmacodynamics
How can we assess the overall effect of an agonist or a drug?
EC 50 : Median effective concentration of a drug or an agonist
% of effect

EC 50
This is known as a “dose-response” curve

Potency
High EC 50 = Low potency
Low EC 50 = High potency
Potency: Amount of drug needed for a given effect
% of effect

Drug A is more potent than Drug B
It takes less of A than B to elicit the same effect
Which drug is more potent, A or B?

Efficacy
Efficacy: maximal effect of a drug
% of effect
Log Drug concentration
100
50
0
A
B
Max effect of B
Max effect of A
B has higher
Efficacy than A
A is a partial agonist
B is a full agonist
Important: a partial agonist (lower efficacy) can have higher potency (lower EC 50 )

Antagonists
Antagonists diminish the effect of an agonist by one of many ways:
Directly compete with agonist binding to receptor
Indirectly block agonist effect:
Block release of the agonist, or block a downstream effect of the agonist
Types of atagonists :
Competitive antagonist
Non-competitive antogonist

Narcan , competitive anatagonist
Naloxone ( Narcan ): A strong competitive inhibitor of opioid receptors
Used to rescue opiate overdose
Competes with binding to the receptor, but does not trigger the same response as
Naloxone

Inhibition by Antagonists
Inhibitors (antagonists) influence the effect of drugs (agonists)
